0: <title>CLIMATE CHANGE AND AGRICULTURE RESEARCH PAPER Environmental impacts of the pork supply chain with regard to farm performance </title>

K. RECKMANN* AND J. KRIETER Institute of Animal Breeding and Husbandry, Christian-Albrechts-University Olshausenstraße 40, 24098 Kiel, Germany (Received 13 May 2013; revised 31 March 2014; accepted 2 May 2014; first published online 27 June 2014)
1: Livestock production is increasingly facing consumer concern about the environmental impact of different production systems. In order to provide a detailed insight into the contribution of pork production, a life-cycle assessment (LCA) of average German pork production was performed. In this way, the global warming potential was estimated at 3.22 kg CO2-eq/kg pork, eutrophication at 23.3 g PO4-eq and acidification at 57.1 g SO2-eq. The functional unit was 1 kg pork as a slaughter weight. The analysis aimed at identifying those farm parameters which had most impact on the LCA results. For that purpose, a deterministic pig farm model was developed which reflected all the processes of a pig farm, while focusing on five distinct areas: general farm information, biological performance, basic feed data, manure management and resource use and emissions. In this way, it was possible to vary the level of performance parameters from farrowing (piglet losses and number of piglets born alive per litter) and finishing stage (lean-meat content, daily weight gain, animal losses and feed conversion ratio). The number of piglets born alive, the lean-meat content and the feed conversion ratio were identified as having the greatest influence on the results. In comparison with average pork production, the values of the impact categories increased and decreased by up to 4.7%. The results show that the fertility of SOWS and the feed management of finishers should be optimized to mitigate environmental impacts at pig farm level.
2: Owing to the rising global food demand, the World Bank estimates that cereal production needs to in- crease by 50%, and meat production by 85%, by 2030 compared to the year 2000 (World Bank 2008). During the last 40 years, European meat consumption has increased by 63% (R. K. Pachauri, personal communi- cation 2014). Pork has been the dominant type of meat (67%) produced in Germany over recent years (Destatis 2013). Although pork has tended to have less environmental impact compared to an equivalent unit of beef or lamb (Kleanthous 2009), the high consump- tion level has demonstrated that the environmental im- pacts arising from its production need to be assessed. Several previous European life-cycle assessments (LCA) have calculated an average global warming potential (GWP) of 3.3 kg CO2-eq/kg pork (Dalgaard et al. 2007; Stephenson 2010; Nguyen et al. 2011;

(2009) mentioned that management practices affect various types of emissions at the same time. Therefore, if the influence of different management strategies on the environmental impacts is to be assessed, it is important to account for the interactions and dependencies of different farm components. This means, when chang- ing one management parameter, all the parameters influenced by this change should be regarded simul- taneously. In the case of pork production, recent studies have identified the farrowing and the finishing stages as having the most environmental impact within the pig housing stage (Kingston et al. 2009; Reckmann et al. 2013), within which the number of piglets born alive per litter, piglet losses, the feed conversion ratio
4: and daily weight gain are known as the most important parameters in terms of economic relevance. When selecting for an increased number of piglets born alive, other aspects such as animal health, animal welfare or environmental impacts should also be considered simultaneously. Hence, it could be relevant to include environmental aspects in future breeding goals. But although Knap (2012) and Shirali et al. (2012) high- lighted that pig breeding has contributed considerably to reduced N emissions from pigs, these aspects have not yet been incorporated into formal breeding goals. Therefore, the aim of the current study was to deter- mine the potential of different production practices at farm level to influence the environmental impacts of pork. In this way, further knowledge should be gained to identify parameters which could potentially be included in future breeding goals. For this reason, various performance parameters in the pig housing stages were examined. All parameters regarded in the current study were calculated separately for piglet production and finishing stage. Furthermore, a pig farm model was implemented whose aim was to account for the afore-mentioned interactions between farm components.
5: Pork production in Northern Germany: a system description

One step of an LCA deals with the inventory of input/ output data, including resources used and substances emitted throughout the entire life cycle of the product under study (ISO 2006a, b). The life-cycle inventory of pork production in Northern Germany is presented in the following sections. A more detailed description of the system is given in the Supporting Information (go to http://journals.cambridge.org/AGS) as well as in Reckmann et al. (2013).

The system under study was the production of pork in the north of Germany in 2010/11. The production process within the system boundaries is illustrated in Fig. 1. The assessment encompassed the production of pre-products, the cultivation of feed crops and the production of feedstuffs as well as the housing of the pigs, and ended with the slaughtering of pigs and processing to pork. Packaging of meat, food retailing and the consumption of meat were ex- cluded. A report from the UK (Kingston et al. 2009) stated that the inclusion of these stages would raise the carbon footprint of UK pork by 4.2%. In accordance

with guidelines published by the British Standards Institution (British Standard Institution 2008), pro- duction units that played a minor role in the production process, such as chemicals and buildings, were not regarded in the current study. The calculation of environmental impacts included primary and secondary sources. Primary sources were on-farm sources, e.g. through feed production, animals and manure management. Secondary sources occurred off-farm, e.g. during the production of fertilizers, pesticides, diesel or feedstuffs produced outside the farm (Rotz et al. 2010).

The inventory started with the production of crops and feed. In this context, six different feed composi- tions were used: feed for lactating and gestating SOWS, piglet feed and three finisher feeds. Table S3 of the Supporting Information, which pro- vides a data inventory per live weight at farm gate.

The next step was to reflect the average pig pro- duction in the marketing year of 2010/11: average farm data from an extension service was used (SSBSH 2011). The main performance parameters are sum- marized in Table S2 of the Supporting Information. The pig housing encompassed all life stages of the pigs and was differentiated into piglet production, weaning and finishing. The inventory for pig housing included the amount of feed needed to raise the pigs, the con- sumption of electricity, heat and water as well as gases emitted. These data together with transport distances of the feed, animals, carcasses and manure are high- lighted in Table S3 (go to http://journals.cambridge org/AGS). Manure was produced by the animals during all stages of pig housing and details of the manure management are described in the Supporting Information. A scheme of the manure management factors included in the current study is illustrated in Fig. S1. It was shown that the application of manure to the field could potentially be a substitute for synthetic fertilizers. The actual study mainly used IPCC guidelines (IPCC 2006) to calculate impacts related to manure management (see Reckmann et al. 2013).

Pigs were transported by lorry to the slaughterhouse at the end of the fattening period. The final product was pork as slaughter weight. Pigs were slaughtered at a live weight of 120 kg, resulting in a slaughter weight of about 95 kg. A data inventory of the slaughtering process is shown in Table S4 of the Supporting Information.
Secondary data were included in databases of the software used, i.e. SimaPro 7.3.3 (Pré Consultants 2009), mainly from the Ecoinvent database V 2.2 (Ecoinvent 2009).

To calculate the environmental impacts due to differ- ent pork production scenarios, a specific calculation procedure was developed. This is illustrated schema- tically in Fig. 2, separated into the base scenario" on the left and 'alternative scenarios" on the right. The approach is described further in the following sections.

4: As illustrated in Fig. 2, the calculation procedure started with the collection of farm data, as described before. Average farm values were used to represent the average pork production in Germany in 2010/11, indicated as the base scenario" in the following.

For the construction of alternative scenarios, farm data from the extension service (SSBSH 2011) were used to calculate quantiles, variances and co-variances of specific performance parameters (see Fig. 2). These parameters were varied separately for the stages of piglet production and finishing. As listed in Table 1, piglet losses and number of piglets born alive per litter were varied in piglet production. Lean-meat content, daily weight gain, animal losses and feed conversion ratio were the parameters varied in the finishing stage.

In a discussion with experts, these parameters were identified as the most important in the pig housing stage as they covered the main biological and feed- related performances. In a first step, the parameters were varied in increments of the quantiles calculated previously (ranging from 10 to 90%). In a second step, variances and co-variances were used together with the parameters varied to calculate joint probabilities (Table 1) with SAS® 9.2 (SAS® Institute Inc. 2008). This procedure was carried out separately for the stages of piglet production and finishing, but jointly for all the parameters of each stage, resulting in a total of 24 alternative scenarios. The mean values listed represented the base scenario, while being equivalent to the 50% quantile. These values were then incorporated into the pig farm model for each scenario.

3: Whole-farm models, as presented by Schils et al. (2007b), are normally a diverse mix of empirical and mechanistic modelling. The deterministic model of the pig farm implemented reflected processes within a farrow-to-finishing farm. The computer simulation used the farm data presented in combination with

emission factors (IPCC 2006) and various estimating equations (e.g. Dämmgen & Hutchings 2008; Sommer et al. 2008; Rigolot et al. 2010). In general, the pig farm model included an integrated production chain with vertical linkages between the four stages: farrowing, weaning, finishing and slaughtering of pigs. Thereby, the pig farm model was divided into five modules: general farm information, biological performances, basic feed data, manure management and resource use and emissions, as shown in Fig. 2. Each of these modules was divided further with regard to the stages farrowing, weaning and finishing. Generally, input parameters were related to biological, economic and environmental variables which were varied within a normal range in the base scenario, as demonstrated by Krieter (2001), whereas economic variables were not considered in the current study. Specifically, the gen- eral farm information described production para- meters reflected in the model. Examples of input data are number of SOWS on the farm, number of piglet places per farm and finisher pigs produced per farm. The data for this area mainly originated from the extension service (SSBSH 2011). The biological per- formance of the farm was addressed in the second module of the pig farm model, considering biological coherencies. Number of piglets born alive per litter, piglet losses, weights of the SOWS, number of piglets produced per sow, N retention of the animals and daily weight gains of the finisher pigs were some of the parameters dealt within this step. The third bullet point included basic feed data, such as feed

intake of the pigs in the different stages, protein content of the feed mixtures and amount of feed needed per farm. The data of this step were provided by the feed company and the extension service. Up to this point, the model was based mainly on Krieter (1994, 2001) and de Vries (1989), but updated with current production parameters and expanded using new aspects. Manure management was described in the fourth module of the pig farm model. A simplified scheme of manure management included in the pig farm model is shown in Fig. S1 of the Supporting Information (go to http://www.journals.cambridge.org. AGS). The consumption of different resources (e.g. heat, electricity, water and transports) as well as emissions (e.g. CH4 from enteric fermentation) was calculated in the last step of the pig farm model. Data sources are listed in Table S3 of the Supporting Information. The model was based on a deterministic approach using fixed probability distributions (e.g. different culling rates for SOWS within cycle, varying litter sizes for SOWS during longevity), according to Krieter (1994, 2001). Interactions between farm para- meters were accounted for since most of the calcula- tions in the pig farm model were interactive. The procedure of a model run is amplified in Krieter (2001). The main steps are explained in the following. The model started with the generation of SOW per- formance. The resulting production cycle was at least 150 or 143 days, whereas the average production cycle was longer due to reproduction failure (e.g. rebreeding, abortion). Litter size and piglet losses were simulated over ten litters with non-linear pattern for both parameters (de Vries 1989). Regarding the above- mentioned performance parameters, an average of 13.7 piglets were born alive per litter and piglet losses averaged 0.15. In the finishing stage, growth per- formance was of major interest and was calculated using the Gompertz function (Krieter & Kalm 1989; Dourmad et al. 1992). Daily weight gain for the base scenario was 788 (see Table S2, Supporting Information): daily weight gain and feed conversion ratio were negatively correlated. A three-phase feeding was used in the finishing stage. Mortality rate in this stage amounted to 0.03. The slaughter weight of C. 95 kg resulted from a final fattening weight of 120 kg and a carcass yield of 0.79. Therefore, the lean-meat content was slightly negatively correlated with feed conversion ratio. In addition to the biological para- meters, the amount of nitrogen (N), phosphorus (P) and methane (CH4) excreted by the pigs was also calculated. Details are described in the Supporting|Information (go to http://www.journals.cambridge.org/ AGS) as well as in Reckmann et al. (2013). A validation of the farm model started during the development process of the individual modules of the model. Their structure, variables and parameters used, and their behaviour were continuously exam- ined. For this type of validation, experts in farm mana- gement and nutrition of the extension services were used to check modules and the system as a whole. Subsequently, the model was subjected to a sensitivity analysis with a systematic investigation of the feedback from the model response to extreme input values. Again, the behaviour of the system was reviewed by the same advisory board and the qualitative expert knowledge was used for optimizing the model. Output An output was generated for each scenario modelled in this way. Among other output values, it included the amount of meat produced, electricity and water use, the amount of manure produced, transportation dis- tances, feed use and on-farm emissions (Fig. 2). The output data were used to estimate the environmental impacts of the pork supply chain for the different scenarios via an LCA. Life-cycle assessment The methodology of LCA was applied because, of all the different methods used to determine the en- vironmental impact of livestock production systems (Halberg et al. 2005; Dalgaard et al. 2007), LCA is widely used, of high relevance and good quality (Thomassen & de Boer 2005). The attributional ap- proach was used in the current study, which describes the environmentally relevant physical flows to and from a life cycle (Curran et al. 2005) in a status quo situation (de Vries & de Boer 2010) in relation to the functional unit (Nguyen et al. 2011). Details of the LCA methodology and its application on pork production are described in Reckmann et al. (2012). All cal- culations for the analysis were performed in SimaPro 7.3.3 (Pré Consultants 2009). 4: Whole-farm models, as presented by Schils et al. (2007b), are normally a diverse mix of empirical and mechanistic modelling. The deterministic model of the pig farm implemented reflected processes within a farrow-to-finishing farm. The computer simulation used the farm data presented in combination with

emission factors (IPCC 2006) and various estimating equations (e.g. Dämmgen & Hutchings 2008; Sommer et al. 2008; Rigolot et al. 2010). In general, the pig farm model included an integrated production chain with vertical linkages between the four stages: farrowing, weaning, finishing and slaughtering of pigs. Thereby, the pig farm model was divided into five modules: general farm information, biological performances, basic feed data, manure management and resource use and emissions, as shown in Fig. 2. Each of these modules was divided further with regard to the stages farrowing, weaning and finishing. Generally, input parameters were related to biological, economic and environmental variables which were varied within a normal range in the base scenario, as demonstrated by Krieter (2001), whereas economic variables were not considered in the current study. Specifically, the gen- eral farm information described production para- meters reflected in the model. The data for this area mainly originated from the extension service (SSBSH 2011). Number of piglets born alive per litter, piglet losses, weights of the SOWS, number of piglets produced per sow, N retention of the animals and daily weight gains of the finisher pigs were some of the parameters dealt within this step. The third bullet point included basic feed data, such as feed

intake of the pigs in the different stages, protein content of the feed mixtures and amount of feed needed per farm. The data of this step were provided by the feed company and the extension service. 6: The environmental impacts of the base scenario are presented in Table 2. The assessment of basic pork production resulted in a GWP of 3.22 kg CO2-eq/kg pork. The EP of pork production in 2010/11 was 23.3 PO4-eq/kg pork, whereas the AP reached 57.1 g SO2 eq/kg pork. It should be highlighted that the GWP was mainly caused by the production of feed (0.63), followed by the housing of pigs (0.30). The slaughter- ing process had a minor share of the GWP (0.07). The most polluting stage in terms of EP was feed production with a share of 0.52. Pig housing was responsible for 0.36, while slaughtering held the smallest share (0.12). The AP was affected mainly by the pig housing stage (0.76); 0.23 of the AP could be traced back to the production of feedstuffs, whereas slaughtering con- tributed considerably less (0.01).

The results of the different levels of piglet losses and number of piglets born alive per litter are shown in Figs 3(a) to (c) for the impact categories (GWP, EP and AP). They illustrate the course of the curve of different parameters assessed to visualize the influence of the changes on the results. The different impact categories showed similar tendencies.
A lower number of piglets per litter (11.1) resulted in a GWP of 3.31 kg CO2-eq, whereas the increase in this parameter (16.3) led to a GWP of 3.16 kg CO2-eq/kg

pork. These values reflected an increase of 2.8% and a decrease of 1.8% compared to the base scenario (Fig. 3(a)). The EP and AP also decreased with a rising number of piglets born alive (23.8-23.0 g PO4-eq and 58.4-56.2 SO2-eq/kg pork), following the same trend as the GWP (Figs 3(b) and (c)).

Rising piglet losses led to a slightly increased GWP, ranging from 3.20 (10% quantile) to 3.24 (90% quan- tile) kg CO2-eq/kg pork. The increase in the EP was less marked, reaching values of 23.2 up to 23.5 g PO4- eq/kg pork (Fig. 3(b)). The variation of piglet losses resulted in a maximum change of the AP by 0.6% (Fig. 3(c)), compared to the base scenario.

Overall, the figures illustrate that variation in the number of piglets per litter affected the impact cate- gories much more than a variation of piglet losses. Additionally, the results of piglet losses showed an almost linear increase. The results of the number of piglets born alive, in contrast, tended to show a non- linear decrease. Starting from the base scenario (50% quantile), a lower number of piglets born alive per litter influenced the results more than higher numbers. Hence, the slope of the curve is higher in the range of 10-50% quantiles than in the range of 50-90% quantiles.

The LCA results of varying performances of finishing pigs for the impact categories are demonstrated in Figs 4(a) to (c). They illustrate the trends of the impact categories for varied lean-meat content, daily weight gain, animal losses and feed conversion ratio.

The estimation of the GWP for a varied lean- meat content resulted in values ranging from 3.31 to 3.14 kg CO2-eq/kg pork. The EP varied within 24.0 and 22.7g PO4-eq/kg (Fig. 4(b)). The results of the AP showed similar changes (58-8-55.4 SO2-eq/kg pork).
The maximal increase or decrease in the GWP for a variation of 10 and 90% quantiles of the daily weight gain was 0.3% compared to the base scenario. The
No. of piglets born alive per litter

Fig. 3. (a) Global warming potential, (b) EP and (c) AP of the piglet production parameters piglet losses and number of piglets born alive per litter. Parameters were varied in units of quantiles, where 50% represents the base scenario.
EP and AP almost showed the same level of changes with values of 23.4-23.3 g PO4-eq and 57.1-57.1 g SO2-eq/kg pork.

The values of the impact categories assessed in con- nection with the animal losses did not differ greatly. The GWP was in the range of 3.20-3.24 kg CO2-eq/kg pork (Fig. 4(a)). The EP and AP showed the same ten- dencies (Figs 4(b) and (c)).

The resulting values from a changed feed conver- sion ratio for the different impact categories showed the highest variations within the finishing parameters, as visible in Figs 4(a) to (c). The GWP differed from 3.09 up to 3.36 kg CO2-eq/kg pork. Hence, a lower feed conversion ratio decreased the GWP by 4.1%, whereas an increased parameter resulted in a 4.2% higher GWP, when compared to the base scenario. The EP and AP had the same trends. The EP resulted in values of 22.4-24.3 g PO4-eq/kg pork. The calculation of the AP finished within a range of 54-4-59-8 g SO2- eq/kg pork.

Altogether, the changes of the lean-meat content and the daily weight gain resulted in an almost linear decrease in the impact categories (Figs 4(a) to (c)), whereas the trend of the lean-meat content was more marked. The changed animal losses resulted in an approximately linear increase in the GWP, but within a small range. The feed conversion ratio, in contrast, increased within the greatest range of the finishing parameters and tended to show a linear trend.

10: <header>RESULTS </header>
Results of the base scenario

The environmental impacts of the base scenario are presented in Table 2. The assessment of basic pork production resulted in a GWP of 3.22 kg CO2-eq/kg pork. The EP of pork production in 2010/11 was 23.3 PO4-eq/kg pork, whereas the AP reached 57.1 g SO2 eq/kg pork. It should be highlighted that the GWP was mainly caused by the production of feed (0.63), followed by the housing of pigs (0.30). The slaughter- ing process had a minor share of the GWP (0.07). The most polluting stage in terms of EP was feed production with a share of 0.52. Pig housing was responsible for 0.36, while slaughtering held the smallest share (0.12). The AP was affected mainly by the pig housing stage (0.76); 0.23 of the AP could be traced back to the production of feedstuffs, whereas slaughtering con- tributed considerably less (0.01).

Farrowing stage

The results of the different levels of piglet losses and number of piglets born alive per litter are shown in Figs 3(a) to (c) for the impact categories (GWP, EP and AP). They illustrate the course of the curve of different parameters assessed to visualize the influence of the changes on the results. The different impact categories showed similar tendencies.
A lower number of piglets per litter (11.1) resulted in a GWP of 3.31 kg CO2-eq, whereas the increase in this parameter (16.3) led to a GWP of 3.16 kg CO2-eq/kg

pork. These values reflected an increase of 2.8% and a decrease of 1.8% compared to the base scenario (Fig. 3(a)). The EP and AP also decreased with a rising number of piglets born alive (23.8-23.0 g PO4-eq and 58.4-56.2 SO2-eq/kg pork), following the same trend as the GWP (Figs 3(b) and (c)).

Rising piglet losses led to a slightly increased GWP, ranging from 3.20 (10% quantile) to 3.24 (90% quan- tile) kg CO2-eq/kg pork. The increase in the EP was less marked, reaching values of 23.2 up to 23.5 g PO4- eq/kg pork (Fig. 3(b)). The variation of piglet losses resulted in a maximum change of the AP by 0.6% (Fig. 3(c)), compared to the base scenario.

Overall, the figures illustrate that variation in the number of piglets per litter affected the impact cate- gories much more than a variation of piglet losses. 12: The present study focused on the most commonly used impact categories: global warming, eutrophication and acidification. However, the LCA methodology used does provide more categories which could potentially be assessed. One currently discussed impact category is land use and it should therefore be dealt with as an example for discussion. Since the global livestock sector already uses around 0.70 of agricultural land (Steinfeld et al. 2006), land use and land use change (LUC) are important parameters which should be incorporated in future assessments (Flysjö et al. 2012). However, Flysjö et al. (2012) highlighted that there is no common methodology yet available to account for land use and LUC. The assessment of four different methods to include LUC showed extremely varying results. This is why most of the hitherto published LCA studies do not include LUC. The present study focused on the pig housing stage, in which land use plays a minor role. Thus, it was decided not to include land use or LUC.

Fig. 4. (a) Global warming potential, (b) EP and (c) AP of the finishing parameters lean-meat content, daily weight gain, animal losses and feed conversion ratio. Parameters were varied in units of quantiles, where 50% represents the base scenario.

Nevertheless, the current authors agree with Flysjö et al. (2012) that it is becoming increasingly important to incorporate emissions related to land use and LUC when further examining e.g. the feeding stage. Recent literature (Stephenson 2010; Meul et al. 2012) has demonstrated that the GWP of various pig diets would increase by 3.9-13.1% when direct LUC (relates to the conversion of land directly attributed to the pro- duction of one or more feed ingredients) was included. Moreover, the inclusion of direct and indirect LUC (indirect LUC accounts for the conversion of land that is induced by changes in production of or demand for one or more feed ingredients) would increase the GWP by 24.4-34.4% (Meul et al. 2012).

Various different whole-farm models already exist to simulate greenhouse gas emissions for dairy farms, such as DairyWise (Schils et al. 2007a), FarmGHG (Olesen et al. 2006), SIMSDAIRY (Del Prado et al. 2011), Integrated Farm System model (IFSM) (Rotz et al. 2012) and FarmSim (Salètes et al. 2004), some of which have been compared in Schils et al. (2007b). But there are only few holistic models available for pork production to date, for instance Farm Assessment Tool (FASSET) (Jacobsen et al. 1998) and MELODIE (Chardon et al. 2012). Most of the models highlighted also account for sustainability farm attributes of biodiversity, land- scape, product quality and animal welfare (Del Prado et al. 2011; Chardon et al. 2012). The pig farm model presented in the current study was a concept to develop a whole-farm model to predict all relevant flows on a farm which potentially influence the sustainability of pork production. It was able to include interactions and dependencies between different farm components. To date, the pig farm model has focused on N and P flows on the pig farm, direct and indirect emissions related to manure management (including in-house and outside storage, application of manure to field and avoided production and application of mineral fertilizers), energy use and animal or farm performances. Since the current study focused on environmental impacts affecting global warming, eutrophication and acidification, this approach could be extended in respect of other assessment aspects. It would be desirable to build similar models for pig farms which include additional parameters, or rather to extend the pig farm model presented. For example, the pig farm model provided a basis for economic aspects, which could be expanded in

future assessments to generate a holistic economic picture of pig farms. Other assessment parameters to be included could be animal welfare, biodiversity or social aspects. Overall, the implementation of the pig farm model was an important step in establishing a basis for the assessment of different management practices.

17: Even if a direct comparison between studies was hindered due to differences in methodological choices (e.g. functional unit, system boundaries, allocation method), the results of the base scenario were in line with those from recent European studies (Dalgaard et al. 2007; Stephenson 2010; Nguyen et al. 2011). Moreover, the results of the alternative scenarios for the piglet production stage highlighted that an increased number of piglets per litter and year could decrease the GWP, EP and AP by up to 1.8%. The decline was due to increased system efficiency, where the impacts of a SOW could be allocated to a higher number of piglets. Even though feed intake rises with increasing number of piglets per sow, more meat is produced with less 'fixed environmental costs' (from g. feed, manure and energy). Thereby, environmental impacts decrease. Regarding the marginal decline of impact category values, it has to be considered that the uncertainty of such results might be relatively high. The level of uncertainty has not been tested in the current study, but it is known that there are actual differences of each scenario, potentially influencing the final results. The analysis showed that the number of piglets born alive increased from 13.7 (base scenario) to 16.3 (90% quantile), resulting in an increase of 18.8% in the amount of meat produced. Thereby, the in- and outputs per kg pork remained almost the same. Only the shares of heating, NH3 emissions from manure, lactating feed and partly finishing feed were affected per kg pork. Kleanthous (2009) confirmed that an increasing number of litters weaned could lower the environmental impacts of the pork supply chain. Even if Kleanthous (2009) used another parameter (number of litters weaned per SOW and year), the fact that the amount of meat increased as well as the feed needed by the SOW to raise the piglets were the same in the present study.

Additionally, the results identified the feed conver- sion ratio as the parameter of the finishing stage which affected the results the most. This was not surprising, since several European LCA studies on pork have

already highlighted feed production as having a considerable impact on the results (Basset-Mens & van der Werf 2005; Basset-Mens et al. 2007; Kingston et al. 2009; Sonesson et al. 2009; Nguyen et al. 2011; Reckmann et al. 2013). The feed conversion ratio was the parameter from the pig housing stage which naturally had the greatest influence on the feed used to produce pork. When comparing inputs and outputs of the base scenario and the 10% quantile of feed conversion ratio, the amount of meat produced increased by only 1%. Despite this, when results were recalculated per kg pork, transports, N in manure, NH3 emissions from manure and the amount of finishing diets used increased slightly. Calculating shared GWP impacts of both scenarios identified that direct emissions, feed for gestating SOWS, finisher feeds and manure use as fertilizer were affected. Therefore, shares of total GWP decreased compared to the base scenario. Moreover, the considerable effect of lean-meat content on the results was not a direct effect on inputs and outputs, but an indirect effect due to the feed conversion ratio being greatly influenced through joint probabilities.
